experiment: F-NK5 falsification-citation confound: length vs epistemic role
hypothesis: Falsification lessons are cited 2.4x more because they are longer (more body text = more mechanical citation opportunities), not because of epistemic role.

method.design: Observational comparison with multiple confound controls
method.falsification_definition: Lessons containing uppercase FALSIFIED anywhere in content (includes PARTIALLY FALSIFIED)
method.non_falsification_definition: Lessons with zero mentions of 'falsif' (case-insensitive) — clean control group
method.inbound_citation_method: grep for \bL-NNN\b across all 829 lesson files, excluding self-citations
method.outbound_citation_method: L-NNN references in Cites: header line
method.sample_strategy: All 48 falsification lessons; 50 non-falsification lessons sampled at uniform intervals from 663 clean controls

word_count.interpretation: No length difference whatsoever — falsification lessons are NOT longer

line_count.interpretation: Falsification lessons are slightly SHORTER by line count

inbound_citations.interpretation: 2.39x raw ratio confirms the 2.4x claim. High variance and skew reduce parametric significance but ratio is robust

length_normalized_citations.interpretation: Length normalization INCREASES the ratio to 2.57x because falsification lessons are slightly shorter

outbound_citations.interpretation: Falsification lessons cite ~2x more (they reference what they falsify), but outbound->inbound correlation is negligible (r=0.08)

correlations.interpretation: Near-zero correlation between length and inbound citations across all groups. Length does not predict citation count

age_confound.interpretation: Falsification lessons are on average YOUNGER (higher lesson numbers), meaning they have had LESS time to accumulate citations — yet are cited MORE. Age works against the falsification group

excluding_L601_outlier.interpretation: L-601 (structural enforcement theorem, 140 citations) accounts for ~40% of the mean difference. Without it, ratio drops to 1.47x but remains elevated

excluding_top3_each_group.interpretation: Still elevated after removing top-3 outliers from each group

era_matched_L500_plus.interpretation: When controlling for age (only recent lessons), the citation advantage is even STRONGER at 2.78x

fraction_with_any_citation.interpretation: Falsification lessons are more likely to receive ANY citation (85% vs 76%)

verdict_detail: The length-confound hypothesis is FALSIFIED. Falsification lessons are NOT longer than non-falsification lessons (0.99x word ratio, r=-0.053 word-citation correlation). The 2.4x citation advantage is driven by epistemic role: falsification lessons invalidate prior claims, creating citation cascades as subsequent lessons must reference the falsification event. Three independent controls (length normalization, age matching, outbound-inbound decorrelation) all point to epistemic role over mechanical length. However, the effect is highly concentrated: L-601 alone accounts for ~40% of the mean difference, and the distribution follows power-law dynamics where a few landmark falsifications attract disproportionate citation. The most conservative estimate (age-matched, outlier-removed) still shows a 1.63x advantage.
nuances: Power-law skew: L-601 (140 citations) dominates. Citation advantage is real but concentrated in landmark results
Median comparison shows no difference (both = 2), suggesting the advantage is in the tail, not the typical falsification
Age works AGAINST falsification group (younger lessons = less time to accumulate), strengthening the epistemic interpretation
Mann-Whitney z=0.505 (not significant) — the rank-order difference is modest; the mean difference is driven by extreme values
Outbound citations are 1.94x higher for falsification lessons (they cite what they falsify) but this does not mechanically produce inbound citations (r=0.08)
